title: Introduction to Programming – Algorithms and Data Handling Quiz

options: `Chosen Magicians: Harry; Ron; Luna; Fred; Ginny;`

explanation.`Chosen Magicians: Ron; Luna; Fred;`: Correct. The `StringBuilder` is constructed with the initial value `"Chosen Magicians:"`, so that prefix is always present in the output. The `forEach` lambda appends only names whose `length()` is strictly less than 5. `Harry` (5) and `Ginny` (5) are excluded; `Ron` (3), `Luna` (4), and `Fred` (4) are included. Each is appended with a leading space and a trailing semicolon, producing `Chosen Magicians: Ron; Luna; Fred;`.
explanation.`Ron; Luna; Fred;`: Incorrect. The initial value passed to the `StringBuilder` constructor is `"Chosen Magicians:"`. Unlike `String` concatenation, `StringBuilder` carries this seed value through all subsequent `append()` calls, so the output always starts with `Chosen Magicians:`.
explanation.`Chosen Magicians: Harry; Ron; Luna; Fre: Incorrect. The condition `magician.length() < 5` filters out names with 5 or more characters. Both `Harry` and `Ginny` have exactly 5 characters, so they do not satisfy the strict less-than check and are not appended.
explanation.` Harry; Ron; Luna; Fred; Ginny;`: Incorrect. The output begins with the seed string `"Chosen Magicians:"` from the constructor, not a blank space. Additionally, `Harry` and `Ginny` are excluded because their length is not less than 5.

questions.source_reference: W09 Algorithms and Data Handling – StringBuilder (constructor, append(), lambda with forEach)

options: The implementation of `equals()` and `hashCode()` follows the consistency rule (equal objects must have the same hash code).
The implementation of `equals()` and `hashCode()` does not follow the consistency rule (equal objects must have the same hash code).
correct_answer: The implementation of `equals()` and `hashCode()` follows the consistency rule (equal objects must have the same hash code).

Correct. The formula `(age + 17) * firstName.hashCode() + lastName.hashCode()` combines all three fields. `percy` has `age = 15` and `firstName = "Percy"`, while `bill` has `age = 21` and `firstName = "Bill"`. Because both `age` and `firstName` differ between the two objects, their hash codes will differ, so the comparison prints `false`.
explanation.The implementation of `equals()` and `ha: Correct. The consistency rule requires that if `a.equals(b)` is `true`, then `a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()` must also be `true`. Here `equals()` considers two `Wizard` objects equal only when all three fields (`firstName`, `lastName`, `age`) are identical. `hashCode()` is computed deterministically from those same three fields. Therefore any two objects that `equals()` deems equal will always produce the same hash code, satisfying the contract.
Incorrect. `percy` and `bill` differ in both `age` (15 vs. 21) and `firstName` (`"Percy"` vs. `"Bill"`). Because `age` is multiplied with `firstName.hashCode()` in the formula, these differences produce distinct hash values, making the equality check `false`, not `true`.
explanation.The implementation of `equals()` and `ha: Incorrect. Both `equals()` and `hashCode()` use the same set of fields (`firstName`, `lastName`, `age`) and compute results deterministically. Any pair of `Wizard` objects considered equal by `equals()` will necessarily yield the same hash code, so the consistency rule is satisfied.

questions.source_reference: W09 Algorithms and Data Handling – Hashing in Java (hashCode(), equals(), consistency rule, hash function design)

learning_objectives: Apply StringBuilder correctly, distinguishing the constructor seed value from subsequent append() calls
Predict the output of lambda-based filtering combined with StringBuilder in a forEach loop
Evaluate whether a custom hashCode() and equals() implementation satisfies the Java consistency rule
Trace a hash function formula across two objects to determine whether their hash codes are equal
